demographic_anchor.name: Geoffrey Caldwell
demographic_anchor.employment: retired — former solicitor (property and commercial law, 35 years)

life_stories.title: A career built on reading carefully
life_stories.event: Geoffrey spent 35 years as a solicitor, specialising first in property law and then commercial contracts. His entire professional skill set was built on reading documents carefully, identifying ambiguity, and never accepting a statement of fact without seeing the underlying evidence. He applied the same analytical standard to news — reading the Times every morning, the Economist weekly, and the FT on Saturdays. He retired five years ago and his morning reading routine has, if anything, expanded.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Extremely careful, analytically rigorous news consumer. Reads primary documents when they're available. Identifies imprecision or overstatement in news articles almost unconsciously. Has high trust in The Times and Economist; lower trust in most broadcast news for analytical depth.

life_stories.title: Brexit — the thing that changed his media diet
life_stories.event: Geoffrey voted Remain and watched the Brexit coverage with a solicitor's eye. He was regularly frustrated by what he saw as imprecision in both Leave and Remain reporting — he kept going to the actual treaty text and EU legislation to understand what commentators were summarising incorrectly. He started reading more primary-source documents and European press (via translation tools) and trusting less of the British interpretive layer. He became a more primary-source-oriented consumer than he had been before.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Primary-source preference intensified. British political journalism is now consumed with a built-in scepticism filter. European and international press viewed as offering useful external frames. Trusts Reuters, the Economist, and FT more than other UK outlets for accuracy.

life_stories.title: Teaching himself to use the internet properly in his fifties
life_stories.event: Geoffrey was a late and reluctant digital adopter. His firm transitioned to digital case management when he was 52 and he found the first two years genuinely difficult. He persisted and became reasonably competent. He now uses tablets and digital subscriptions fluently, but he doesn't use social media at all — not from principle, but from simple lack of interest. He found the interfaces irritating and the content low-quality and never went back.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Digitally competent but not digitally native. Uses digital news infrastructure well (apps, subscriptions, email newsletters) but not social media or video platforms for news. YouTube is not in his information ecosystem.

news_avoidance_frequency.label: never — high civic duty and low time pressure

avoidance_trigger.label: no avoidance — manages frustration through source curation

primary_platform.label: The Times (digital) + The Economist + FT Saturday + BBC Radio 4

youtube_news_use.label: negligible — not in his information ecosystem

mainstream_media_trust.label: moderate-high — The Times and Economist specifically

bbc_trust_level.label: moderate-high — BBC Radio 4 specifically trusted; TV news less so

paid_subscription_held.label: The Times digital, Economist, FT (print Saturday)

ai_news_attitude.label: resistant — solicitor's concern about accountability and verification chain

coping_mechanism.description: Geoffrey's legal training has made primary-source verification a reflex. When coverage seems imprecise, he goes to the underlying document. He manages information quality by going deeper, not by avoiding.

key_tensions: Very high analytical standards make him a demanding news consumer — trusts few outlets enough to accept conclusions without checking
Remain-voting in a media landscape he perceives as dominated by Brexit-cheerleading titles creates ongoing source quality frustration

narrative: I spent 35 years reading contracts and finding the places where imprecision would become a problem later. That doesn't switch off when you retire. I read The Times every morning — digitally now, though I miss the physical newspaper more than I expected — the Economist on Thursdays, and the FT on Saturdays. BBC Radio 4 is on in the background most of the morning. I trust Reuters wire reports more than almost any British interpretation of them. Brexit was educational for me in that regard — I kept going back to the actual treaty text because the commentary in the British press, on both sides, was consistently imprecise or selective. I'm not on social media. I tried Twitter briefly during the pandemic, found the quality intolerable, and didn't go back. YouTube I don't use for news — I might watch a documentary about something if my wife suggests it, but that's different. I'm not going to get my understanding of tax policy from someone talking to a camera in a home studio. The BBC I trust more than I probably should — I have a lot of respect for Radio 4 specifically, and I think their overseas correspondents are among the best journalists working. Their domestic political coverage I think has become more equivocal than the situation sometimes warrants. I'll pay for quality journalism indefinitely. The model of 'free but supported by clicks' has produced exactly the journalism you'd expect.
decision_bullets: Primary sources are The Times digital, Economist, FT, and BBC Radio 4 — all paid or public-funded.
BBC Radio 4 specifically trusted; BBC TV news viewed as less rigorous.
YouTube is not used for news — views it as incompatible with analytical rigour standards.
No social media — brief trial period demonstrated quality was below his acceptable threshold.
News avoidance is non-existent — high civic duty, low time pressure, and strong reading habit.
Pays for multiple quality news sources without hesitation — views it as normal cost of informed citizenship.
AI news summaries rejected — solicitor's instinct: automated synthesis lacks verifiable accountability chain.
Brexit shifted him toward primary-source-first consumption and away from interpretive British journalism.

core_memory.identity_statement: Retired solicitor, Winchester, £62k household, analytical news consumer, Times/Economist/FT subscriber, no social media, BBC Radio 4 daily.

life_defining_events.event: Legal career formed habit of primary document verification over interpretive summaries
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Applies legal reading standards to journalism; highly sceptical of conclusions without evidence

life_defining_events.event: Brexit coverage frustration drove primary-source-first news habit
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: British interpretive journalism consumed with built-in scepticism filter; Reuters/FT preferred

life_defining_events.event: Late digital adoption — competent user but not social media participant
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Digital subscriptions and apps used fluently; social media and YouTube absent from ecosystem

key_influencers: Times columnists he has read for decades

immutable_constraints.budget_ceiling: no price ceiling for quality journalism
